title: Structural Modifications: Anchors, LOD, and Improved Rendering
content:
## Beyond Per-Gaussian Optimization

The methods covered so far optimize each Gaussian independently. A more powerful approach exploits **correlations between Gaussians**. Nearby Gaussians in a smooth surface tend to have similar attributes — similar colors, similar orientations, similar scales. Structural modifications make these correlations explicit, enabling dramatically more efficient encoding.

## ScaffoldGS: Anchor-Based Representation

ScaffoldGS (Lu et al., CVPR 2024) introduces the concept of **anchors** — sparse control points that each predict the attributes of \(K\) nearby Gaussians.

Each anchor carries:
- 1 position (3 floats)
- 1 scale (3 floats)
- \(K\) spatial offsets (\(K \times 3\) floats) — where the \(K\) Gaussians are relative to the anchor
- 1 feature vector (\(F\) floats) — a learned descriptor

A small MLP takes (anchor feature, view direction, distance to camera) and predicts the Gaussian attributes (scale, rotation, color, opacity) for all \(K\) Gaussians at once.

**Why this helps compression**: instead of storing 59 independent floats per Gaussian, you store \(F\) features per anchor + a small MLP. The MLP itself must be encoded, but it's shared across all anchors — one fixed overhead. Anchor positions also exhibit strong spatial correlations (they tend to lie on smooth surfaces), making them more compressible than arbitrary Gaussian positions.

## CompGS: Anchor-Based with Affine Warping

CompGS predicts Gaussians from anchors using an **affine warp**:

\[\text{Gaussian}_k = \text{AffineWarp}(\text{anchor attributes}, \mathbf{f}_{anchor} + \mathbf{f}_{splat_k})\]

where \(\mathbf{f}_{splat_k}\) is a per-Gaussian residual feature. The warp is a linear transformation (predicted by a small MLP) rather than a full nonlinear mapping. **Residuals** from the anchor prediction are smaller and more compressible than raw attributes.

## HAC and HAC++: Hash-Grid Context

HAC (Hash-grid Assisted Context, Chen et al., ECCV 2024) extends ScaffoldGS by introducing a spatial hash grid (InstantNGP-style) over the anchor positions:

1. Hash anchor position \((x,y,z)\) to look up a learnable feature vector from a hash table.
2. The hash feature predicts the **distribution parameters** for arithmetic coding of anchor attributes — effectively a learned prior.
3. Training with a rate term \(\lambda R\) makes the learned distributions match the actual data, minimizing coded bit-rate.

HAC++ extends this to achieve approximately **100× compression** of the original 3DGS representation while maintaining competitive quality.

**Key insight**: the hash grid is a continuous spatial model — nearby anchors share hash bucket features, so the model implicitly learns spatial correlations in the distribution of attribute values. This is much more powerful than coding each attribute independently.

## OctreeGS: Hierarchical Level-of-Detail

OctreeGS organizes anchors in an octree — a hierarchical spatial partition where each node contains an anchor at the center of its voxel. Coarser octree levels correspond to larger voxels and coarser Gaussian representations.

This enables **LOD rendering**: for distant regions, use anchors from coarser octree levels (fewer, larger Gaussians). For nearby regions, use fine-level anchors. This:
- Reduces rendering cost for large scenes
- Reduces aliasing from undersampled geometry
- Enables progressive streaming (coarse level first, then refine)

OctreeGS training proceeds coarse-to-fine: start with coarse anchors, then progressively densify into finer octree levels.

## Improved Rendering: EWA Splatting and Post-Processing

The standard tile-based rasterizer is an approximation of exact perspective-correct splatting. Two approaches improve quality:

**EWA Splatting / PerspectiveSplat**: applies the elliptical weighted average filter correctly in perspective space, reducing aliasing at oblique viewing angles. With more geometrically accurate splats, fewer Gaussians are needed to achieve the same visual quality — directly reducing the parameter count.

**SUNDAE UNet post-processing**: after rendering, apply a small UNet to refine the output image. By offloading some reconstruction quality to the post-processor, fewer Gaussians are needed in the explicit representation. Trade-off: the UNet adds inference cost and must be encoded alongside the Gaussians.
